coredata.dc:title: High-temperature RGB ultralong afterglow carbon dots based on cavity encapsulated by cyclodextrins for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Bingyan
Guo, Chunyang
Wang, Fengdi
Wang, Jiao
Liu, Haiyan
He, Gaohong
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have the disadvantages of short lifetime, single wavelength and poor stability. Phosphorescence usually suffers severe thermal decay due to strong non-radiative transitions caused by temperature increasing, limiting the use of CDs. Herein, α-Cyclodextrin provides hydrophobic cavities, so for CDs can be encapsulated into this cavity. OH on cyclodextrin also reacts to generate covalent bonds. Both of them restricts the rotation and vibration of the benzene ring and reduces the non-radiative transitions. Compared to the matrix-limited domain, the cavity encapsulation has a better wrapping effect, while the hydrophobic cavity can better isolate the O2 quenching and stabilize the triplet excitons. Based on the abovementioned considerations, the blue/green/red naked-eye visible afterglows of the obtained RGB(Red-Green-Blue) -CDs lasted for about 16 s, 28 s and 2 s. At the same time, due to the high-temperature resistant cavity structure of cyclodextrins, G-CDs can realize high-temperature phosphorescence for 4 s at 200 °C, better than any of the high-temperature phosphorescent CDs reported to date. This is also the first report of CDs emitting red phosphorescence at 200 °C. The differences in lifetime and wavelength among three RGB afterglow CDs achieve potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High-temperature phosphorescent
Cavity encapsulate
Ultra-long lifetime RGB afterglow
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of as-synthesized RGB-CDs; (b) Photographs of the RGB-CDs with a 254 nm UV lamp and the UV lamp was turned off.

(a–c) Fluorescence spectra (black line) and afterglow spectra (red, green and blue lines) of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs; (d) CIE coordinates of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs; (e) Time-resolved afterglow decays and fitted curves of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs at 340, 275 and 280 nm excitation, respectively; (f) Afterglow emission spectra of B-CDs in 77–477 K. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Structural characterization of RGB-CDs. (a–c) TEM images of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs (The inset shows the particle size distribution); (d) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs; (e) XPS spectra of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs; (f–i) C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and B 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and B 1s in R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs.

(a) 1H NMR spectra of 9-phenanthrenylboronic acid, G-CDs without Urea; (b) Fluorescence and afterglow emission processes of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs simulated by calculation; (c) Afterglow time of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs that can be observed by the naked eye after the 254 nm UV excitation light is turned off by keeping them at 373 k, 423 k and 473 k for 30 min; (d–f) Time-resolved afterglow decays and fitted curves of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs at 373 k, 423 k and 473 k.
RGB-CDs for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications (a) Digital encryption; (b) Graphical encryption.
Fluorescence lifetime (τf), fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY, Φf), phosphorescence lifetime (τp) and phosphorescence quantum yield (PhQY, Φp) of G-CDs without Urea and G-CDs.

head.ce:title: High-temperature RGB ultralong afterglow carbon dots based on cavity encapsulated by cyclodextrins for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have the disadvantages of short lifetime, single wavelength and poor stability. Phosphorescence usually suffers severe thermal decay due to strong non-radiative transitions caused by temperature increasing, limiting the use of CDs. Herein, α-Cyclodextrin provides hydrophobic cavities, so for CDs can be encapsulated into this cavity. OH on cyclodextrin also reacts to generate covalent bonds. Both of them restricts the rotation and vibration of the benzene ring and reduces the non-radiative transitions. Compared to the matrix-limited domain, the cavity encapsulation has a better wrapping effect, while the hydrophobic cavity can better isolate the O2 quenching and stabilize the triplet excitons. Based on the abovementioned considerations, the blue/green/red naked-eye visible afterglows of the obtained RGB(Red-Green-Blue) -CDs lasted for about 16 s, 28 s and 2 s. At the same time, due to the high-temperature resistant cavity structure of cyclodextrins, G-CDs can realize high-temperature phosphorescence for 4 s at 200 °C, better than any of the high-temperature phosphorescent CDs reported to date. This is also the first report of CDs emitting red phosphorescence at 200 °C. The differences in lifetime and wavelength among three RGB afterglow CDs achieve potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have excellent optical properties such as better fluorescence, upconversion luminescence and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) due to the strong quantum size effect. These luminescent properties have shown significant application prospects in optoelectronic devices, energy storage, sensing, anti-counterfeiting, imaging, medical, biological applications and other fields[1–14]. Particularly, the RTP CDs have an afterglow emission performance which is very attractive because of their prolonged emission lifetime, larger Stokes shift and minimized interference from short-lived auto-fluorescence and scattered light. Now it has shown a broad application prospect in anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, sensing and other advanced fields. However, due to the triplet excitons in relation to the spin-forbidden transition it is much harder to achieve the RTP of CDs than fluorescence. More importantly, the triplet exciton is readily exhausted by vibrational deactivation and O2 quenching under room or higher temperature [15–20]. Thus, in order to obtain effective RTP, it is vital to improve the rate of intersystem crossing (ISC) by enhancing the spin–orbit coupling of excitons or to stabilize the triplet-excited states via using structural confinement.

Embedding CDs within organic or inorganic matrices is a widely employed method to achieve RTP. These matrices are essential for stabilizing the long-lived ternary state by effectively isolating and rigidifying CDs, minimizing the non-radiative leap of the triplet exciton and avoiding collisions of CDs with oxygen. At present, RTP afterglow of CDs have commonly been carried out within rigid matrices. For example, Lu's group utilized the urea rigid matrix confinement to achieve a panchromatic high-brightness four CDs RTP. The maximum lifetime and quantum yield reached 2141 ms and 56.55 %[21]. Xu's group synthesized CDs composites using the rigid structure of boronic acid as a matrix and exploiting its inorganic electronic defects. They exhibited RTP under 365 nm excitation with an average afterglow lifetime of 347.96 ms[22]. Wang et al exploited the rigid environment of cyanuric acid and the covalent bonds formed between CDs and cyanuric acid to realize the dual-mode afterglow emission. Green TADF (Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence) is visible to the naked eye for 5 s and NIR RTP is visible to the naked eye for up to 2.5 s[23]. Liu's group utilized a PVA substrate to form hydrogen bonds with the carbonyl group on the surface of CDs to rigidify the carbonyl group and achieve TADF and RTP bimodal afterglow emission centered at 456 nm and 569 nm[24].

Cyclodextrins are cyclic oligosaccharides that contain at least six d-(+)-glucopyranose units linked by α-(1, 4) glucosidic bonds. The three natural CDs, α-, β-, and γ-Cyclodextrins, have 6, 7, and 8 glucose units, respectively. As a kind of cage-type molecule, they have a hydrophilic exterior and a hydrophobic inner cavity[25–28]. Therefore, they can encapsulate other molecules, allowing a wide range of hydrophobic guest molecules to interact with the inner cavity of the macrocycle to form inclusion complexes. Cyclodextrins’ encapsulation capacity and flexibility allow host-guest-type interactions to modify the physical, chemical, and biological properties of the guest molecules[29–32]. If luminescent molecules or materials are encapsulated in cyclodextrins, on one hand, its inherent hydrophobic cavity can inhibit molecular non-radiative transitions, which is conducive to luminescence enhancement; on the other hand, it can also effectively isolate the entry of external oxygen and impurities in order to protect the tri-linear exciton, and achieve the emission of RTP afterglow. For example, Liu's group confined terephthalic acid to the cavity of β-Cyclodextrin, and the rigid environment suppressed nonradiative decay with afterglow emission at 410 nm and a quantum yield of 23.99 %[17]. Li et al constrained 4-biphenylboronic acid within the cavity of β-Cyclodextrin and utilized intermolecular hydrogen bonding to achieve an ultra-long-lived RTP in water for 1.03 s[33]. Compared to the matrix-limited domain, the cavity encapsulation has a better wrapping effect, while the hydrophobic cavity can better isolate the O2 contact and stabilize the triplet excitons, which facilitates the phosphorescence emission. When the temperature increases, the matrix vibrates more violently and the confinement effect decreases. Conversely, the cavity also maintains a good rigid structure at high temperatures, which provides the possibility of emitting phosphorescence at high temperatures.

It is worth noting that phosphorescence usually suffers severe thermal decay due to strong non-radiative transitions induced by temperature increase, which is more severe for organic covalent compounds with lower melting points. However, higher than room temperature is unavoidable in many practical applications, for example, optoelectronic devices, where the luminescent properties of luminescent materials are much reduced. So far, reports on phosphor materials with high-temperature resistance are scarce. In 2024, Ma's group developed a strategy for high-temperature phosphorescence using planar rigid molecules as guests and rigid polymers as the main substrate. The doped material exhibits a 40 s afterglow at 293 K, a 20 s afterglow at 373 K, and a 6 s afterglow at 413 K[34]. Huang synthesized yellow-doped materials using diarylfluoro[3,2-b]pyridine as the guest molecule and polyacrylic acid as the host molecule. It emited a green high temperature phosphorescent (HTP) with a 2-second afterglow at 373 K and has a delayed lifetime of 208 ms[35]. Wang obtained a series of matrix-free fluorinated boron nitride dots by constructing a rigid F-induced hydrogen bonding network. F-BNDs(e) were heated up to 150 °C and still retained an afterglow lifetime of up to 0.25 s[36]. Qu synthesized CDs@BA with bimodal RTP-TADF afterglow using levofloxacin in solid-fused boric acid. As the temperature of the composite changes from 93 K to 373 K, the afterglow color gradually changes from yellow to blue[37]. Therefore, it is crucial to explore strategies for developing high-temperature, high-performance phosphorescent materials. Undoubtedly, phosphorescent materials with high temperature resistance not only enrich the phosphorescent system, but also realize more application scenarios, such as high-temperature luminescence and temperature sensing.

In this work, α-Cyclodextrins provide hydrophobic cavity to encapsulate 1-pyrenylboronic acid of, the precursor of CDs, and then restrict the motion of the benzene ring. Moreover, covalent bonds between boron hydroxyl group and hydroxyl groups from α-Cyclodextrin can reduce non-radiative transitions. The dual effects stabilize the triplet-excited states. At the same time, since the B atom has empty p orbitals that can accept electrons, it is easy to form p-π conjugation with the benzene ring by reducing the minimum unoccupied orbital energy level of CDs, and then decrease the energy gap between T1 and S1 (ΔEST) [38]. Moreover, urea is also introduced to increase the rigidity of the matrix and introduce a large number of CO, which can promote effectively the ISC process [39,40]. By increasing the conjugation of the precursor, RG-CDs were synthesized. Meanwhile, due to encapsulation capacity of α-Cyclodextrin, it can restrict the movement of molecules under high-temperature environments as well, which provides the possibility of the afterglow emission of CDs at high temperature. The CDs that emit red afterglow are named R-CDs, those that emit green afterglow are named G-CDs, and those that emit blue afterglow are named B-CDs.

RGB-CDs were synthesized by microwave method using α-Cyclodextrin, urea and 1-pyrenylboronic acid, 9-phenanthrenylboronic acid and 4-methyl ester phenylboronic acid, respectively (Fig. 1 a). As-synthesized RGB-CDs appeared as a white powder, and as shown in Fig. 1b, all of them emitted a blue fluorescence under the UV lamp of 254 nm, when the UV lamp was turned off, the blue/green/red afterglow of RGB-CDs lasted for about 16 s, 28 s, and 2 s visible to the naked eye, respectively (Fig. 1b).

The fluorescence and RTP emission wavelengths of R-CDs are 400 and 600 nm with excitation wavelengths at 254 and 340 nm, respectively, shown in Fig. 2 a. Similarly, in Fig. 2b, G-CDs is 375 and 505 nm with excitation wavelengths of 254 and 275 nm, respectively. But B-CDs has the same fluorescence and RTP emission wavelengths at 410 nm with excitation wavelengths at 254 and 280 nm, shown in Fig. 2c. The phosphorescence excitation spectrum of R-CDs (Fig. S1) shows that 340 nm is the optimal excitation wavelength. The afterglow spectra of G-CDs and B-CDs under different excitation lights (Figs. S2, S4) show the green 505 nm and blue 410 nm afterglows, the intensity of the afterglow vary with different excitation light, and the best afterglow emission is exhibited under the excitation light of 275 nm and 280 nm. The results of the phosphorescence excitation spectra of GB-CDs (Figs. S3, S5) are consistent with Figs. S2 and S4. R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs all showed non-excitation-dependent. The CIE coordinates (Fig. 2d) show that the R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs afterglow emissions are located at (0.63, 0.37), (0.18, 0.47) and (0.15, 0.02), respectively.

Since the fluorescence and the afterglow emission peak of B-CDs overlap at 410 nm, variable temperature afterglow spectroscopy (Fig. 2f) was performed to further verify the afterglow mechanism. In the range of 277–477 K, the intensity of the afterglow emission at 410 nm was enhanced with increasing temperature, which is a typical feature of TADF. In addition B-CDs exhibited a phosphorescence characteristics at 77 K–227 K, with the afterglow intensity decreasing with increasing temperature, and this also proved that the blue afterglow emitted by N@B-CDs was blue TADF[4,41].

TEM images in Fig. 3 a, b and c show the R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs are spherical. Their average particle sizes are 4.20, 3.75 and 3.70 nm in the particle size distribution graph, respectively. Figs. S6–S8 are AFM of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs, from which it can be seen that the size of the CDs in the Z-direction are 3.5–4.5 nm, and the AFM and TEM together demonstrate that the CDs are a 3.5–4.5 nm spherical structure. FT-IR and XPS are performed to further determine the functional groups and chemical composition of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs. The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs display the stretching vibration peaks of N CO, CO, CN, OH and NH, and triazine ring at 1440, 1650, 2150, 2940 and 3300 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 3d) [42]. N CO, CO and NH belonging to urea appear in RGB-CDs. XPS (Fig. 3e) shows that RGB-CDs contain four elements: B, C, N and O. Fig. 3f–i show that the high-resolution C1s spectrum deconvoluted to obtain four peaks at 284.8, 286.6, 287.9 and 289.4 eV belonging to CC, CO, OCN and OCN2, respectively [43]. Urea introduces the element N to the CDs, and then the peak of N appears. A new bond, BN, which belongs to neither urea nor the precursors of CDs or α-Cyclodextrin, appears. The XPS spectrum of N1s exhibits two peaks at binding energies of 398.3 (BN) and 399.8 eV (CN). The high-resolution O1s spectrum contains two peaks at 531.5 and 533.0 eV corresponding to OCN and CO/CO. The XPS spectra of B1s with peaks at binding energies 190–191 eV are BN [44]. The above FT-IR and XPS spectra prove that R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs all contain CN and CO, and it could reasonably be inferred that COH from α-Cyclodextrin and BOH from the precursors of CDs react with NH2 of urea, and then form the BN and CN.

For the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 4 a), the hydrogen peaks of 9-phenanthrenylboronic acid, as exemplified by the G-CDs without Urea, are all progressively shifted upfield in the cyclodextrin system, indicating that a shielding effect occurs due to encapsulation into the α-Cyclodextrin's cavity, which is consistent with previous reports[20,45]. The 1H NMR spectra of R-CDs without Urea and B-CDs without Urea showed the same results (Figs. S9, S10). If α-Cyclodextrins were replaced by β-d-glucose or methyl-β-Cyclodextrin during the synthesis process, afterglow almost disappered, shown in Fig. S11. β-d-glucose does not have cavity structure. Although methyl-β-Cyclodextrin also has a cavity structure, the benzene ring cannot enter into the cavity of cyclodextrin due to the steric hindrance of methyl group. It proved that the cavity and cavity size play crucial roles in the synthesis process of the CDs. The cavity of cyclodextrins provides a more rigid microscopic environment for the CDs, and then the cavity can well restrict the rotation of the benzene ring [45]. At the same time, the BOH reacts with the OH on the cyclodextrin to generate a covalent bond, which restricts the vibration of the benzene ring to inhibit its non-radiative transitions, so that the luminescence performance of the CDs is enhanced [46]. R-CDs without Urea, G-CDs without Urea and B-CDs without Urea were presented as white powder at room temperature and all emitted blue fluorescence under 254 nm UV light. When the UV light was turned off, the red, green and blue afterglows of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs lasted for 1.5 s, 12 s and 10 s, respectively (Fig. S12). When urea is added, the generation of BN introduces a large amount of CO into the system, and then it is favorable to promote the ISC process of CDs, so that more singlet excitons are transferred to excited triplet excitons, which is favorable to the emission of phosphorescence. It is worth mentioning that due to the hydrophobicity of the cavities of cyclodextrins, it can isolate oxygen and stabilize the triple-state excitons, which is conducive to the emission of phosphorescence. Therfore, as shown in Fig. S13, the afterglow intensity of GB-CDs are improved by 45 %, 23 % and 547 %, compared to RGB-CDs without Urea, respectively. As shown in Fig. S14, the fluorescence intensity of RG-CDs was attenuated by 70 % and 29 %, whereas that of B-CDs was enhanced by 267 %. This is due to the fact that the afterglow of B-CDs is TADF, and the fluorescence is enhanced as the electrons move from S1 to T1 through the ISC process, and then jump back to S0 after returning to S1 through RISC (Reverse Intersystem Crossing), and are released as fluorescence. It can be seen that the emission peaks of both fluorescence and phosphorescence did not change after the addition of urea, which indicates that the addition of urea did not greatly affect the structure of the CDs.

The unpaired electrons on the N atom can be used as electron donors to enhance the luminescent properties of CDs, while B, as an electron-withdrawing atom, can be used to promote the RTP process by nicely lowering the ΔE and making the ISC process more likely to occur[47,48]. Meanwhile, the empty p-orbital of B can attract the π-electrons on the benzene ring to form a p-π conjugation, which reduces the minimum empty orbital energy level of the CDs. The energy gaps (ΔEST ) between the singly excited state (S1) and the triplet excited state (T1) of R-CDs and G-CDs were calculated to be 1.0 and 0.9 eV (Fig. 4b), respectively. ΔE = hv = hc/λ ≈ 1240/λ (eV) (1), ΔEST = ΔES − ΔET (2). ΔEST is calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2). Although the red R-CDs and the green G-CDs possess large ΔEST values but still emit RTP; therefore, the exciton can be shifted from the excited singlet state to a higher excited triplet state. Conversely, smaller ΔEST of blue B-CDs facilitates the promotion of the ISC process as well as RISC process, therefore, and produces the TADF process through S1 back to S0. In addition, the afterglow quantum yields of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs were 0.72 %, 6.40 % and 60.70 %, respectively. Time-resolved afterglow decays of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs were shown in Fig. 2e, and their average lifetimes were calculated to be 216.30 ms, 1.53 s and 962.98 ms, according to the equation
τ a v g = ∑ α i τ
i 2
/ ∑ α i τ i
[8]. The large amount of CO in the CDs due to the addition of urea also facilitates the promotion of the ISC process, resulting in a higher QY (quantum yield) and longer lifetime RTP[49,50]. Taking the green phosphorescent G-CDs and G-CDs without Urea as examples, their lifetimes were calculated from their fluorescence or afterglow decay curves, as shown in Fig. 2e and Figs. S15–S17. The addition of urea reduced the FLQY of G-CDs from 61.98 % to 45.10 %, shown in Table 1 . This is because the large amount of CO contained in urea promotes the ISC process, resulting in a decrease in the process from S1 to S0 and an increase in the process from S1 to T1. Although the PhQY decreased from 8.30 % to 6.40 %, the afterglow lifetime increased from 1.38 s to 1.53 s, and the naked-eye afterglow was significantly enhanced from 12 s to 28 s.

It is well known that triplet excitons suffer from severe thermal decay, and there are few reports about phosphorescent materials with high-temperature resistance. After being kept in a 373 K atmosphere for 30 min, afterglow of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs were visible for 0.5 s, 7 s and 2 s after the 254 nm excitation light was turned off, respectively (Fig. 4c). When the temperature rises to 423 K, the afterglows were visible for 0.2 s, 5 s and 1 s. Moreover, even at a high temperature 473 K, the afterglow can still maintain 0.1 s, 4 s and 0.2 s, respectively. Fig. 4d-f show the afterglow decay curves of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs at 373 K, 423 K and 473 K. The relative afterglow lifetimes are labelled in the figures. The afterglow lifetime of the three CDs decreases with increasing temperature. This is because the increase in temperature leads to strong non-radiative leaps and the energy is released more in the form of vibration. However, the cavity structure can confine the CDs very well, which can reduce the non-radiative leaps and make them have good afterglow emission even at temperatures up to 200 °C. Our work is better than any of the high-temperature phosphorescent CDs reported so far, and for the first time, red phosphorescent emission at 200 °C is presented. Apparently, R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs exhibit excellent high-temperature resistance. This proves cavity structure with the rigid matrix and can well restrict the vibration and rotation of the molecules even in relatively high-temperature environments [51].

Given their ultralong lifetime and excellent tunable RTP performance, RGB-CDs can be applied to high-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption based on time and wavelength resolutions. Fig. 5 a shows that under a 254 nm UV lamp, a blue luminescence number “8888″ is presented because both G-CDs without Urea and G-CDs emit blue fluorescence. After turning off the UV lamp, the green light-emitting number ”8888″ is visible at 5 s because both them emit green phosphorescence. However, due to the long phosphorescence lifetime of the green G-CDs, the strong green luminescence number “2024″ can be maintained at 25 s. For the blue afterglow B-CDs without Urea and B-CDs, they showed similar results. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5b, when irradiated with a 254 nm UV lamp, the patterns ”flower“, ”stem“ and ”leaf“ all emit blue fluorescence. And due to the different phosphorescence lifetimes and colors of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs, red flowers, green leaves, and blue stems could be seen for 1 s after stopping the light source, and the flowers and stems gradually disappeared at 10 s and 15 s. These results clearly demonstrate a great potential of CDs in advanced security applications through a special manner for information readout.

In summary, the cavity structure of α-Cyclodextrin can be well utilized to restrict the rotation and vibration and reduce the nonradiative leaps of CDs. Urea successfully form rigid matrix with α-Cyclodextrin, and then BN bond and a large amount of CO promote the ISC process, so that the singlet excitons become triplet excitons more often, thus generating the ultralong lifetime RTP. By increasing the degree of conjugation of the precursors, CDs emitting RGB three-color afterglows can be obtained, not by energy transfer. It is worth mentioning that, due to the high-temperature-resistant structure of α-Cyclodextrin, G-CDs can still have 4 s of green phosphorescence at up to 200 °C. Based on the characteristics of RGB afterglow CDs with ultra-long lifetime and high stability, the information encryption and anti-counterfeiting have been successfully realized, and their practical application in high-temperature luminescence has also been made possible.

Bingyan Han: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Chunyang Guo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Fengdi Wang: Funding acquisition. Jiao Wang: Writing – original draft. Haiyan Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Gaohong He: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Liaoning Key Laboratory of Chemical Additive Synthesis and Separation (ZJKF2309).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.02.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Defects and structural limitation-induced carbon dots–silica hybrid materials with ultralong room temperature phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Chao, J. Wang, X. Dong, J. Ren, H. Zhang, R. Song, Z. Xie, Defects and Structural Limitation-Induced Carbon Dots–Silica Hybrid Materials with Ultralong Room Temperature Phosphorescence, The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters 13(41) (2022) 9558-9563.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi‐color pure organic room temperature phosphorescent materials with long lifetime and high efficiency
